metric_name: NonStop VLDB backup rate (4 backup vendors)
metric_value: 400–750 GB/hour
notes: All four backup software vendors plus SAP R/3 reported backup results in 400-750 GB/hour range.

metric_value: 3-16% in tests
notes: Low CPU utilization confirmed minimal production impact during backup operations.

metric_value: 1.5 TB/hour (with 1-2 additional TLIOP channels)
notes: Estimated 1.5 TB/hour achievable by adding 1-2 TLIOP channels and additional tape drives.

metric_value: ~10 GB/hour
notes: Industry standard backup rate of ~10 GB/hour in 1997; NonStop VLDB represents 40-75x improvement.

metric_name: Practical database size limit at 10 GB/hour
metric_value: 40-200 GB (depending on update frequency)
notes: At 10 GB/hour, databases over 40-200 GB faced backup windows exceeding available downtime.

metric_value: AlphaServer 8400; 8 CPUs; 8 GB RAM; TLIOP with 4 PCI buses; 30 SCSI controllers; 16 tape drives
notes: Benchmark hardware configuration documented in detail.

metric_value: Oracle, Informix, Sybase, Microsoft SQL Server, SAP R/3
notes: NonStop VLDB compatible with major RDBMS and enterprise applications including SAP.

metric_value: High-end Unix or cost-effective Windows NT
notes: Solution supported both Unix and NT OS options.

metric_value: Parallelized online backup reduced but did not eliminate bottleneck
notes: Aberdeen documented the 10-year-prior advance (parallelized online backup) and its limitations.

metric_value: Less than 10 terabytes (data warehouses with few updates)
notes: Aberdeen noted even the largest 1997 databases were under 10 TB.

metric_value: 4 categories: administrative costs, availability reduction, downtime after crashes, database sclerosis
notes: Aberdeen structured the backup/restore problem into 4 categories of organizational drag.

metric_value: 5 new applications: reconfiguration, rolling backup, data migration, disaster tolerance, security recovery
notes: Aberdeen identified 5 novel applications enabled by fast backup/restore beyond traditional use.

metric_name: Primary deployment targets for fast backup/restore
metric_value: Enterprise backup centers, data warehouses, rapidly-scaling new solutions (ERP, web servers)
notes: Aberdeen recommended deploying fast backup/restore first to data centers, data warehouses, and fast-growing ERP/web servers.

metric_value: Night-long backup → 30 minutes; 2-day weekend backup → 3 hours
notes: Conservative 20x speedup calculation based on NonStop VLDB 400 GB/hour vs prior 10 GB/hour.

metric_value: Under 20 minutes for databases under 100 GB
notes: Aberdeen calculated that at 400 GB/hour, 95%+ of 1997 databases could be restored in under 20 minutes.

metric_value: First-mover; ½ to 1-year technology lead
notes: Aberdeen estimated Digital had a ½ to 1-year lead over competitors; lead based on hardware tuning complexity requiring 6+ months minimum.

metric_value: Well-regarded service arm for installation and maintenance
notes: Digital offered not just software/hardware but configuration and maintenance services.

metric_name: Aberdeen's bottom line on NonStop VLDB
metric_value: Technology is real; has arrived; delivers significant benefits; demands strategic rethinking
notes: Aberdeen strongly endorsed NonStop VLDB as real technology with profound architectural implications.

metric_value: Backup/restore will go from commodity to strategic capability
notes: Aberdeen predicted backup/restore would transition from infrastructure overhead to strategic IS toolset.

metric_name: SGI as first competitor after Digital
metric_value: SGI among first competitors in fast backup/restore market
notes: Aberdeen predicted SGI, HP, and Sun would be first non-Digital entrants; Intel-based suppliers (Sequent) to follow.

metric_value: Upper limits to all databases removed; even 10TB can be backed up in one night
notes: Aberdeen predicted fast backup/restore would remove practical database size limits.

metric_value: Fast backup/restore enables dedicated migration server
notes: Aberdeen predicted emergence of migration server combining backup/restore with data-migration software.

metric_value: Periodic backup to remote tape bank viable alternative to expensive mirroring
notes: Aberdeen predicted fast backup/restore could provide disaster tolerance within 1-10 minute gap at far lower cost than full mirroring.

metric_name: NonStop VLDB discontinued after DEC acquisition
metric_value: Compaq's acquisition of DEC in June 1998 effectively ended NonStop VLDB product development and marketing as predicted.
notes: Compaq acquisition of DEC in June 1998 effectively ended NonStop VLDB product development and marketing. | NonStop VLDB discontinuation post-Compaq acquisition confirmed.

metric_name: Fast backup/restore achieved via SAN and deduplication
metric_value: SAN snapshots, disk-based backup, and deduplication (EMC Data Domain, NetApp SnapVault) delivered order-of-magnitude improvements in backup/restore speed by 2005-2010, validating Aberdeen's prediction.
notes: The backup/restore speed improvement Aberdeen predicted was realized through SAN (EMC, NetApp), disk-to-disk backup, and deduplication (EMC Data Domain, Quantum) — not through optimized tape as DEC proposed. | Fast backup/restore via SAN/deduplication confirmed. EMC Data Domain (acquired by EMC 2009) and NetApp disk-based backup validated prediction.

metric_value: Symantec acquired Open Vision Technologies' backup software in 1999; it became Symantec NetBackup, one of the dominant enterprise backup products for decades.
notes: Open Vision's backup software became Symantec NetBackup, one of the dominant enterprise backup products for decades. | Source notes confirm Symantec/Open Vision acquisition resulting in NetBackup product.

metric_value: EMC acquired Legato Systems for $1.3 billion in 2003. Legato NetWorker became part of EMC and later Dell Technologies portfolio.
notes: Legato NetWorker (tested with NonStop VLDB) was acquired by EMC; later became part of Dell Technologies portfolio. | Source notes confirm EMC/Legato acquisition 2003 for $1.3B.

metric_value: Aberdeen's prediction that backup/restore would 'go strategic' was validated: continuous data protection (CDP), cloud backup (AWS Backup, Azure Backup), and cloud DR became multi-billion dollar markets by 2015-2020.
notes: Aberdeen's prediction that backup/restore would 'go strategic' was validated: continuous data protection (CDP), cloud backup (AWS Backup, Azure Backup), and cloud DR are now core enterprise architecture elements. | Strategic backup/restore validation confirmed by cloud backup market growth.

metric_name: SGI failed to enter fast backup/restore
metric_value: SGI went bankrupt 2006/2009 and never became a backup/restore technology leader. The fast backup market was led by disk-based backup vendors (EMC, NetApp, Symantec), not traditional HPTC vendors.
notes: Aberdeen predicted SGI as first competitor entrant. SGI went bankrupt instead; the fast backup market was led by disk-based backup vendors, not traditional HPTC hardware suppliers. | SGI backup market failure confirmed by bankruptcy and market history.

metric_value: Data warehouse refresh frequency improved dramatically. Modern data warehouses and data lakes support near-real-time or continuous loading by 2010-2020, validating Aberdeen's prediction.
notes: Aberdeen's prediction of more frequent data warehouse refresh was validated; modern data warehouses and data lakes support continuous loading and real-time analytics. | Data warehouse refresh frequency improvement validated by modern OLAP/data lake architectures.
